# What Happened to Indigo?

**Source:** [Color Nerd](https://www.youtube.com/@ColorNerd1) (YouTube Shorts)
**Date:** 2024-12-17
**URL:** https://www.youtube.com/shorts/bg_5p6oTaUU
**Views:** 7,650

## Description

Why indigo sometimes gets left out of the rainbow — tracing the transition from Newton's ROYGBIV to modern 6-color spectral naming, driven by von Bezold in 1874.

## Transcript

What happened to indigo? Why does it sometimes get left out of the rainbow? Ultimately I blame a German dude named von Bezold.

Up until the mid-19th century, physicists discussing the spectrum mainly followed Isaac Newton's scheme — ROY G BIV. But as the technology of prisms improved, physicists like Wollaston and Fraunhofer got better images of the solar spectrum and started noticing more details, like these absorption bands caused by different elements in the atmosphere blocking certain wavelengths. They started naming and charting those, and those lines became benchmarks for scientists who wanted to describe the colors of the spectrum and where they began and ended — because everybody's vision is a little bit different, and it's useful to have benchmarks.

And this brings us to von Helmholtz. He published a couple volumes on optics in the 1850s and 60s and is credited with establishing modern color science. In a precursor to the CIE chromaticity diagram, Helmholtz divided up the colors of the spectrum and wrapped them around an arch. One goal of this diagram was to demonstrate more clearly which hues were complementary — or added up to white. This meant diverging from Newton's ROY G BIV to add non-spectral purple under the arch, and to make it clear that both orange and yellow had types of blue as their complements. For orange: cyan blue (in his words) — and this is the color that Newton would have just called "blue." And opposite yellow: indigo.

But Helmholtz was perhaps the last major physicist to use indigo as a spectral color.

In 1866, Ernst Wilhelm von Brücke published this wheel which used the term "blue" for yellow's complement — no indigo here. And in 1874, Wilhelm von Bezold officially killed indigo as a spectral color. Here's the passage: "The blue which extends from here to near the line G used to be called indigo blue in physics up to the present, but as it is in fact perfectly impossible to imitate it by indigo, while it can be rendered by ultramarine, we shall here designate it as ultramarine blue."

## Key Facts

- **Newton's ROYGBIV** — 7-color spectrum scheme dominated physics until mid-1800s
- **Fraunhofer lines** — improved prism technology revealed absorption bands; became wavelength benchmarks
- **Helmholtz** (1850s–60s) — established modern color science; last major physicist to use "indigo" as spectral color; precursor to CIE chromaticity diagram with spectral arch + non-spectral purple
- **von Brücke** (1866) — dropped indigo, used "blue" for yellow's complement
- **von Bezold** (1874) — officially killed indigo: renamed it "ultramarine blue" because the spectral color couldn't actually be imitated by indigo dye, but could be matched by ultramarine pigment
- **What Newton called "blue"** ≈ what we now call cyan
- **What Newton called "indigo"** ≈ what we now just call blue
